evidence: Large callback-bag runtimes are used as primary seams: `RuntimeContainerOps`, `RuntimeUrbitOps`, `RuntimeConfigOps`, and related structs in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`, plus `importerRuntime` in `goseg/importer/importer.go` and `startupInitRuntime`/`startBackgroundServicesRuntime` in `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`.

Multiple constructors are thin wrapper chains with little policy added (`New*`, `Default*`, `with*`, `resolve*`), e.g. `goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go`, `goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go`, `goseg/broadcast/state.go`, `goseg/rectify/rectify.go`, and `goseg/docker/events/events.go`.

Several interfaces are exposed mainly as indirection seams with only one concrete flow in-batch, such as `C2CModeOrchestrator` implemented by `c2cModeFlow` in `goseg/system/wifi_c2c_policy.go`, while most behavior is still coupled to global defaults and package-level singletons.

Session lifecycle responsibilities are split and duplicated between goseg/session/client_manager.go and goseg/session/connection_registry.go; both implement near-identical broadcast loops and connection-state mutation paths.
Urbit transition handling in goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go mixes config mutation, Docker lifecycle control, Startram API calls, and click command orchestration in one runtime file.

Configuration mutation logic is spread across multiple switch/registry mechanisms: goseg/config/config_update_schema.go (patch key parsing/apply), goseg/config/urbit.go (section mutator switch), and goseg/structs/configs.go (large custom UnmarshalJSON key switch).

Multiple tests mutate package-global runtime dependencies directly (for example defaultAPIClient/defaultConfigService in goseg/startram/state_sync_test.go and goseg/startram/backup_runtime_test.go), creating shared mutable test seams instead of scoped dependency injection.

Transition publish APIs explicitly return typed failures (`ErrTransitionBusFull`, `ErrTransitionPublishTimeout`) in `goseg/docker/events/events.go`, but multiple callsites ignore publish errors entirely.

Error wrapping strategy is inconsistent across core paths: `goseg/config/urbit.go` repeatedly uses `%v` in `fmt.Errorf`, while nearby config parsing code in `goseg/config/config_update_schema.go` uses `%w` and preserves causal chains.

Failure handling in telemetry paths is mixed: `goseg/system/metrics/metrics.go` suppresses several read/parse errors and continues with partial results, while sibling system runtime code commonly returns wrapped errors immediately.

findings.summary: Function-field runtime bags have become god abstractions that hide domain boundaries and force defensive nil-check plumbing.
evidence: `runtime_ops.go` defines many layered ops structs composed mostly of function pointers, then composes them again (`runtimeSeamRegistry`, `defaultRuntime*` builders).

`importerRuntime` carries a very wide set of unrelated callbacks (storage, auth, shipworkflow, docker, filesystem, ACME, config), then `ensureForWorkflow` performs long nil-validation chains before execution.
`startBackgroundServicesRuntime` and `startupInitRuntime` similarly aggregate many independent subsystem callbacks, indicating boundary collapse into orchestration bags.
findings.suggestion: Split runtime bags by cohesive use-cases (e.g., upload-storage, ship-lifecycle, startram-sync) and pass small typed collaborators into workflows; keep a thin composition root that wires concrete dependencies once.

findings.summary: Repeated `New/Default/with/resolve` wrapper chains add call depth without adding meaningful policy.
evidence: `wifi_runtime_methods.go` has `NewWiFiRuntime`, `DefaultWiFiRuntime`, `defaultWiFiRuntime`, `withWiFiRuntime`, `NewWiFiRuntimeWith`, `mergeWiFiRuntime` where several functions only forward to another.
`broadcast/state.go` exposes `newBroadcastStateRuntime`, `NewBroadcastStateRuntime`, `DefaultBroadcastStateRuntime`, `resolveBroadcastStateRuntime`, then many APIs resolve defaults again internally.
`rectify.go` and `events.go` repeat the same shape (`New*`, `default*`, `with*`, delegate/default runtime) with minimal behavior per layer.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on one constructor + one explicit override path per module (e.g., `NewX(opts)`), remove redundant forwarding helpers, and move default selection to the composition root instead of per-call resolve helpers.

findings.summary: Global default runtime singletons undermine interface boundaries and make behavior depend on hidden mutable process state.
evidence: `defaultEventRuntime`, `defaultBroadcastStateRuntime`, and `defaultWiFiRuntimeService` are package globals used by default-path APIs.
`handler/ship/backup.go` publishes transitions through package-level function variables that capture `events.DefaultEventRuntime()`, mixing global singleton usage with mutable test seams.
Default-resolve helpers across modules implicitly choose global instances when no override is passed, reducing explicit dependency flow.
findings.suggestion: Inject long-lived runtime instances from startup wiring into handlers/services and remove package-global default mutable seams; keep globals only for immutable constants.

findings.summary: Some exported interfaces are abstraction-first rather than use-case-first, with only one concrete implementation in-batch.
evidence: `C2CModeOrchestrator` is exported, but in this batch `newC2CModeFlow`/`newC2CModeFlowWithDependencies` always return the single `c2cModeFlow` implementation.
`main.go` calls `system.NewC2CModeFlow().EnterC2CMode()` and does not consume any alternate implementation capability beyond the concrete flow behavior.
findings.suggestion: Either make `c2cModeFlow` concrete/internal and inject dependencies directly for tests, or define a narrower interface at the call site that is consumed by multiple real implementations.

findings.summary: Session connection state and broadcast behavior are duplicated across manager and registry layers, blurring ownership boundaries.
evidence: client_manager.go defines sessionBroadcaster.broadcast with token/client iteration, write failure handling callback, and LastActive mutation.
connection_registry.go also defines registry.broadcast with nearly the same token/client iteration and LastActive mutation behavior.

ClientManager still performs state mutation paths (transitionClientState, removeConnectionByConnLocked) while ConnectionRegistry also owns similar domain state transitions, so orchestration and data-layer behavior are intermixed.
findings.suggestion: Choose a single owner for broadcast and session-state transitions (prefer the registry/store layer), expose a focused interface for the manager, and remove duplicated broadcast iteration logic.

findings.summary: Urbit operations runtime centralizes many unrelated responsibilities, reducing cohesion and making transitions harder to reason about.
evidence: urbit_operations_runtime.go handles alias/domain validation, config persistence, Docker start/create/cleanup actions, MinIO account creation/linking, schedule configuration, and transition step wiring in one file.
Functions like buildToggleMinIOLinkSteps orchestrate click operations, API/service calls, and config updates in one transition builder, combining domain and infrastructure concerns.

runtime_ops.go already exposes seam structs (RuntimeUrbitConfigOps, RuntimeContainerOps), but urbit_operations_runtime.go still calls concrete package functions directly (docker, click, startram), bypassing a consistent orchestration abstraction.
findings.suggestion: Split urbit transition handling into focused components (config mutation, container lifecycle, integration side effects) behind injected runtime interfaces, then compose transitions from those components.

findings.summary: Configuration schema translation is implemented in multiple disconnected mapping systems, creating drift-prone update paths.
evidence: config_update_schema.go defines a patch registry and key-specific parse/apply logic for many system fields.
config/urbit.go separately maps section enums to mutator function types via UrbitSectionMutator switch cases.
structs/configs.go implements a large manual UnmarshalJSON switch for UrbitDocker field keys and defaults, creating a third independent schema mapping path.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate schema/key mapping into one authoritative mapping layer (generated or declarative), and reuse it for patch parsing, section mutation, and JSON translation to prevent parallel schema logic.

findings.summary: Tests repeatedly mutate package globals for dependency control, indicating inconsistent test seam design and coupling between tests.
evidence: state_sync_test.go captures/restores defaultAPIClient, defaultConfigService, and defaultRetrieveStateSyncer in multiple tests using package-global replacement.
backup_runtime_test.go performs similar global replacement patterns for defaultAPIClient/defaultConfigService/defaultBackupInfrastructureService.
importer_test.go manipulates package-global uploadSessions/uploadDir/tempDir and relies on global DefaultEventRuntime channels, reinforcing shared mutable test setup patterns.
findings.suggestion: Introduce constructor-scoped test runtimes and per-test dependency injection containers so tests avoid mutating package globals and can run with isolated state.

findings.summary: Large groups of thin pass-through wrappers create interface surface sprawl without clear behavioral boundaries.
evidence: token_service.go re-exports many tokens package types/constants/functions and adds only small guard checks, while preserving nearly identical API names and flow.
runtime_ops.go defines many tiny op structs plus default constructor functions that mostly forward package functions into fields, producing a broad seam surface with limited policy encapsulation.
The combined pattern increases places where behavior is declared versus where it is actually implemented, making ownership and change impact less obvious.
findings.suggestion: Collapse wrappers to a smaller boundary-focused API: keep only wrappers that enforce policy/translation, and group remaining dependencies into fewer cohesive runtime facades.

findings.summary: Transition publish failures are silently dropped in workflow and backup paths.
evidence: `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go` uses `_ = defaultEventRuntime.PublishUrbitTransition(...)` in `newWorkflowRuntime`, discarding broker backpressure/cancellation failures.
`goseg/handler/ship/backup.go` calls `publishUrbitTransitionForBackupService(...)` repeatedly without checking returned errors (loading/success/failure/clear events).
`goseg/docker/events/events.go` defines concrete publish failures (`ErrTransitionPublishTimeout`, `ErrTransitionBusFull`), so dropping returns loses intended failure signaling.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared helper for transition publication that either returns errors to callers or consistently logs and classifies dropped events; replace ignored publish calls in workflow and backup handlers with that helper.

findings.summary: Multiple modules stringify underlying errors, breaking `errors.Is/As` behavior.
evidence: `goseg/config/urbit.go` returns wrapped IO/JSON failures with `%v` (for example load/decode/create/rename paths), losing causal chains.
`goseg/handler/ship/backup.go` builds text messages and returns `fmt.Errorf("%s", text)` instead of preserving source errors.
`goseg/config/config_update_schema.go` already uses `%w` for parse/registry failures, so error propagation style is inconsistent inside adjacent config codepaths.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on `%w` whenever re-emitting a concrete upstream error, and only use string-only errors for true user-facing validation cases with no source error. Refactor backup handlers to return wrapped source errors plus structured transition messages.

findings.summary: Metrics collectors mix fail-fast and silent-degrade strategies without a consistent contract.
evidence: `GetTemp` in `goseg/system/metrics/metrics.go` suppresses many file/parse errors with warnings and may still return success when partial data exists.
`getDiskLabel` in the same file swallows label lookup/read failures by returning empty values, while `GetDisk` hard-fails on mount/statfs errors.
`goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go` generally propagates contextualized errors immediately, showing a different failure contract for sibling system runtime operations.
findings.suggestion: Define and document one telemetry error policy (strict fail-fast or explicit partial-result contract). If partial results are intended, return structured partial-error metadata (or joined warnings) instead of silent suppression.

findings.summary: Docker client init failure in network runtime returns raw error without operation context.
evidence: `KillContainerUsingPort` in `goseg/docker/network/network.go` returns raw `err` when client creation fails, unlike other methods in the same file that wrap with operation context.

`goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard.go` consistently wraps failures with contextual messages (`failed to build`, `failed to read`, `unable to create`), highlighting inconsistency in nearby runtime infrastructure.
findings.suggestion: Wrap `DockerClientNewFn` failures in `KillContainerUsingPort` with operation context (port and action), matching the rest of the runtime error style.
